text:
Yup, useful energy output is the foundation of the economy just like sunlight is the foundation of the ecosystem. 

To first approximation, any given country’s good & services production will be proportionate to their energy output.
embedded_text:
People keep missing this.

GDP is NOT FIXED

GDP = Joules * GDP/Joule

Increasing our energy supply and using that energy more efficiently makes the world wealthier.

Trump does not appear to benefit privately from the Boeing 747-8 jet gifted by Qatar. Here's a detailed breakdown: 1. **Ownership and Use of the Jet**: The post by @amuse clarifies that the jet is being accepted by the Department of Defense (DOD) on behalf of the U.S. government, not Trump personally. It is intended to be used as an exhibit in Trump’s presidential library, which will be managed and owned by the National Archives and Records Administration (NARA). Presidential libraries, while often associated with a former president, are public institutions maintained by NARA, not private assets of the president. Therefore, the jet does not become Trump’s personal property, nor does it directly benefit him or his family financially or otherwise. 2. **Emoluments Clause Considerations**: The Emoluments Clause of the U.S. Constitution (Article I, Section 9, Clause 8) prohibits federal officeholders from accepting gifts from foreign governments without congressional consent, to prevent undue influence. @amuse notes that Congress has previously authorized the Secretary of Defense to accept gifts on behalf of the Air Force, which aligns with legal protocols. Since the jet is being accepted by the U.S. government (via the DOD) and not Trump personally, this arrangement does not appear to violate the Emoluments Clause. The jet's eventual placement in a presidential library further supports the idea that it serves a public, historical purpose rather than a private benefit to Trump. 3. **Context of the Gift**: The web results indicate that the jet is being considered for use as a new Air Force One, a role that serves the office of the president, not the individual occupying it. While Trump may have toured the jet and expressed interest in it (as noted in the CNBC and AP News reports), this does not equate to private ownership or benefit. The jet’s modification and use are being handled by governmental entities, such as the DOD and potentially L3Harris Technologies, as part of official operations. 4. **Public and Online Perceptions**: Some replies to the X post, like those from @vileTexan and @EndWokeness (not directly quoted but referenced in the broader context), raise concerns about potential surveillance risks or geopolitical implications of accepting a jet from Qatar. However, these concerns are speculative and do not directly address whether Trump benefits privately. They instead reflect broader skepticism about the deal’s implications, which @amuse counters by emphasizing the legal framework and governmental oversight. 5. **Historical Precedent**: Presidential libraries often house significant artifacts, including aircraft, from a president’s tenure. For example, the Ronald Reagan Presidential Library displays a retired Air Force One plane (a Boeing 707). These exhibits are not considered private benefits to the former president or their family but are instead public assets meant to preserve history. The same logic applies to the Qatari jet in Trump’s case. ### Conclusion: President Trump does not privately benefit from the jet gifted by Qatar. The plane is being accepted by the U.S. government, will be managed by the DOD, and is destined for a presidential library under NARA’s oversight. This arrangement aligns with legal protocols, including congressional authorization for such gifts, and does not appear to violate the Emoluments Clause. LFP is the chemistry currently being deployed at scale for grid storage. https://t.co/Jarm6fhwAs

It can start tunneling within 48 hours of arriving, and it’s fast — over 1 mile per week. That’s 6 times faster than the last model, though still slower than a snail. But not for long. Prufrock’s next goal? Beat 1/10th of human walking speed, or 7 miles per day. To get there, it mines continuously, installs tunnel walls on the go, and uses rubber wheels instead of old-school rails. Earth is 4.5 billion years old.
Life on Earth is 3.7 billion years old.

In just ~0.5 billion years, the Sun’s growing luminosity will destroy the carbon cycle, and with it all complex life.

text: Neuralink is partnering with UAE and Cleveland Clinic!
embedded_text:
🇦🇪 We’re launching our first clinical trial in the Middle East at Cleveland Clinic Abu Dhabi in partnership with the Department of Health Abu Dhabi!

The UAE-PRIME trial explores how individuals with motor and speech impairment can use thought to control devices & communicate. https://t.co/hRzjCObzOk

I see this misinterpreted all the time.

Elon is not literally proposing that we build a single giant solar farm to power the whole country.

It’s an illustration.

“what about all the transmission you’d need!??”

You’re missing the point entirely. Solar PV has a higher power

South Africa has now passed 142 laws forcing discrimination against anyone who is not black!

Even though I was born in South Africa, the government will not grant @Starlink a license to operate simply because I am not black.

This is a shameful disgrace to the legacy of the great Nelson Mandela who sought to have all races treated equally in South Africa.

{"__typename": "Tweet", "card": {"legacy": {"binding_values": [{"key": "unified_card", "value": {"string_value": "{\"component_objects\":{\"details_1\":{\"type\":\"grok_share\",\"data\":{\"profile_user\":{\"id_str\":\"44196397\",\"profile_image_url_https\":\"https:\\/\\/pbs.twimg.com\\/profile_images\\/2035314704307081216\\/71U1ftM3.jpg\",\"name\":\"Elon Musk\",\"screen_name\":\"elonmusk\",\"highlighted_label\":{\"description\":\"X\",\"badge\":{\"url\":\"https:\\/\\/pbs.twimg.com\\/profile_images\\/1955359038532653056\\/OSHY3ewP_bigger.jpg\"},\"url\":{\"urlType\":\"DeepLink\",\"url\":\"https:\\/\\/twitter.com\\/X\"},\"userLabelType\":\"BusinessLabel\",\"userLabelDisplayType\":\"Badge\"},\"verified\":false,\"is_blue_verified\":true},\"grok_user\":{\"id_str\":\"1720665183188922368\",\"profile_image_url_https\":\"https:\\/\\/pbs.twimg.com\\/profile_images\\/1893219113717342208\\/Vgg2hEPa.jpg\",\"name\":\"Grok\",\"screen_name\":\"grok\",\"highlighted_label\":{\"description\":\"xAI\",\"badge\":{\"url\":\"https:\\/\\/pbs.twimg.com\\/profile_images\\/1769430779845611520\\/lIgjSJGU_bigger.jpg\"},\"url\":{\"urlType\":\"DeepLink\",\"url\":\"https:\\/\\/twitter.com\\/xai\"},\"userLabelType\":\"BusinessLabel\",\"userLabelDisplayType\":\"Badge\"},\"verified\":false,\"is_blue_verified\":true,\"verified_type\":\"Business\"},\"id\":\"eVeUFVjYQkCJvK7JWgc6kF2D9\",\"conversation_preview\":[{\"message\":\"Are his calculations accurate?\",\"sender\":\"USER\",\"grokMode\":\"NORMAL\",\"mediaUrls\":[]},{\"message\":\"Let\\u2019s break down Jesse Peltan\\u2019s calculations and claims in the target post (Post ID: 1919470705906729320) to evaluate their accuracy. The post discusses the physics of heat transfer, focusing on radiative heat loss from the human body, and makes specific numerical claims about heat flux, human body heat output, and the benefits of insulation. I\\u2019ll analyze each key calculation and claim, referencing established physics principles and the context provided by the related posts and web results.\\n--\\nHeat Loss Percentages from the Human Body\\nClaim: \\\"In typical conditions, only ~15% of the human body's heat loss is from convection. ~60% is from radiation, and ~20% is from evaporation (breathing and sweating).\\\"\\nAnalysis:\\nThese percentages align with the web search result from Quora, which cites the National Library of Medicine: approximately 60% radiation, 22% evaporation, and 10\\u201315% convection and conduction, with conduction being the smallest at about 2%. Peltan\\u2019s numbers are consistent with this breakdown.\\nThe exact percentages can vary depending on environmental conditions (e.g., air temperature, humidity, clothing, and activity level). For instance, in a dry environment, evaporation might increase, while in a still, cold room, radiation dominates even more. However, the cited values are standard for a resting human in a typical indoor setting (around 20\\u201325\\u00b0C, moderate humidity).\\nVerdict: This claim is accurate and aligns with established physiological data.\\n--\\nRadiative Heat Flux Calculations\\nClaim: \\\"If your skin temp is 34\\u00b0C (93.2\\u00b0F), you emit 505 W\\/m\\u00b2. A wall at 10\\u00b0C (50\\u00b0F) emits 364 W\\/m\\u00b2 while a wall at 40\\u00b0C (104\\u00b0F) emits 545 W\\/m\\u00b2.\\\"\\nAnalysis:\\nPeltan is using the Stefan-Boltzmann law to calculate radiative heat flux, which states:\\n\\\\[\\nq = \\\\varepsilon \\\\sigma T^4\\n\\\\]\\nWhere:\\n\\\\( q \\\\) is the radiative heat flux (W\\/m\\u00b2),\\n\\\\( \\\\varepsilon \\\\) is the emissivity (assumed to be 1.0 for human skin and walls, as noted in the Wikipedia web result: \\\"Human skin has an emissivity of very close to 1.0\\\"),\\n\\\\( \\\\sigma \\\\) is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant (\\\\( 5.67 \\\\times 10^\\u007b-8\\u007d \\\\, \\\\text\\u007bW\\/m\\u007d^2\\\\text\\u007bK\\u007d^4 \\\\)),\\n\\\\( T \\\\) is the absolute temperature in Kelvin.\\na) Skin at 34\\u00b0C (307 K):\\n\\\\[\\nT = 34 + 273 = 307 \\\\, \\\\text\\u007bK\\u007d\\n\\\\]\\n\\\\[\\nq = 1.0 \\\\times 5.67 \\\\times 10^\\u007b-8\\u007d \\\\times (307)^4\\n\\\\]\\n\\\\[\\n(307)^4 = 307 \\\\times 307 \\\\times 307 \\\\times 307 = 8,876,316,241\\n\\\\]\\n\\\\[\\nq = 5.67 \\\\times 10^\\u007b-8\\u007d \\\\times 8,876,316,241 = 503.3 \\\\, \\\\text\\u007bW\\/m\\u007d^2\\n\\\\]\\nPeltan\\u2019s value is 505 W\\/m\\u00b2, which is very close. The slight difference could be due to rounding (e.g., skin temperature might be approximated as 34.1\\u00b0C, or a slightly different value for \\\\( \\\\sigma \\\\)).\\nb) Wall at 10\\u00b0C (283 K):\\n\\\\[\\nT = 10 + 273 = 283 \\\\, \\\\text\\u007bK\\u007d\\n\\\\]\\n\\\\[\\n(283)^4 = 283 \\\\times 283 \\\\times 283 \\\\times 283 = 6,417,344,761\\n\\\\]\\n\\\\[\\nq = 5.67 \\\\times 10^\\u007b-8\\u007d \\\\times 6,417,344,761 = 363.9 \\\\, \\\\text\\u007bW\\/m\\u007d^2\\n\\\\]\\nPeltan\\u2019s value is 364 W\\/m\\u00b2, which matches exactly within rounding.\\nc) Wall at 40\\u00b0C (313 K):\\n\\\\[\\nT = 40 + 273 = 313 \\\\, \\\\text\\u007bK\\u007d\\n\\\\]\\n\\\\[\\n(313)^4 = 313 \\\\times 313 \\\\times 313 \\\\times 313 = 9,595,293,241\\n\\\\]\\n\\\\[\\nq = 5.67 \\\\times 10^\\u007b-8\\u007d \\\\times 9,595,293,241 = 544.1 \\\\, \\\\text\\u007bW\\/m\\u007d^2\\n\\\\]\\nPeltan\\u2019s value is 545 W\\/m\\u00b2, again very close, with the difference attributable to rounding.\\nVerdict: These radiative heat flux calculations are accurate and correctly apply the Stefan-Boltzmann law. The minor discrepancies are within acceptable rounding errors.\\n--\\nHuman Body Heat Output and Net Cooling Requirement\\nClaim: \\\"Your body is ~100 W and ~2 m\\u00b2. You need a net cooling of ~50 W\\/m\\u00b2 to maintain your body temperature.\\\"\\nAnalysis:\\nTotal heat output: The human body at rest produces about 100 W of heat (a widely accepted average for basal metabolic rate, equivalent to about 80\\u2013120 W depending on size and activity). Peltan\\u2019s estimate of 100 W is reasonable.\\nSurface area: The average human body surface area is approximately 1.5\\u20132 m\\u00b2 (commonly cited as 1.8 m\\u00b2 for an average adult). Peltan\\u2019s approximation of 2 m\\u00b2 is on the high side but acceptable.\\nHeat flux per unit area: If the body produces 100 W over 2 m\\u00b2, the average heat flux is:\\n\\\\[\\n\\\\text\\u007bHeat flux\\u007d = \\\\frac\\u007b100\\u007d\\u007b2\\u007d = 50 \\\\, \\\\text\\u007bW\\/m\\u007d^2\\n\\\\]\\nPeltan claims a net cooling of 50 W\\/m\\u00b2 is needed to maintain body temperature, which matches this calculation exactly. However, this is a simplification because:\\n  - The 50 W\\/m\\u00b2 is the total heat flux the body must lose, not just the radiative component. Since radiation is ~60% of heat loss (as per Peltan\\u2019s earlier claim), radiative heat loss would be:\\n  \\\\[\\n  50 \\\\times 0.6 = 30 \\\\, \\\\text\\u007bW\\/m\\u007d^2\\n  \\\\]\\n  - The net radiative heat flux depends on the difference between what the body emits and what it absorbs from the environment. If the body emits 505 W\\/m\\u00b2 (as calculated) and absorbs 364 W\\/m\\u00b2 from a 10\\u00b0C wall, the net radiative loss is:\\n  \\\\[\\n  505 - 364 = 141 \\\\, \\\\text\\u007bW\\/m\\u007d^2\\n  \\\\]\\n  Over 2 m\\u00b2, this is:\\n  \\\\[\\n  141 \\\\times 2 = 282 \\\\, \\\\text\\u007bW\\u007d\\n  \\\\]\\n  This is much higher than the 60 W (30 W\\/m\\u00b2 \\u00d7 2 m\\u00b2) needed for radiative heat loss, suggesting the body would lose heat too quickly in this scenario, which aligns with Peltan\\u2019s point about needing to be \\\"bathed in infrared radiation\\\" to avoid excessive cooling.\\nVerdict: The 100 W and 2 m\\u00b2 assumptions are reasonable, and the 50 W\\/m\\u00b2 total heat flux is correct. However, the net radiative heat loss calculation oversimplifies the balance between emission and absorption, though it supports Peltan\\u2019s qualitative argument about the importance of surrounding temperatures.\\n--\\nInsulation\\u2019s Impact on Energy Use\\nClaim: \\\"Doubling the amount of insulation in a home cuts the energy required to maintain a given air temperature in half, but it also cuts the differential between the temperature of the walls and the air.\\\"\\nAnalysis:\\nEnergy reduction: The claim that doubling insulation halves energy use for maintaining air temperature is based on the physics of heat transfer through conduction. The heat loss through a wall is given by:\\n\\\\[\\nQ = \\\\frac\\u007bkA \\\\Delta T\\u007d\\u007bd\\u007d\\n\\\\]\\nWhere:\\n  - \\\\( Q \\\\) is heat loss (W),\\n  - \\\\( k \\\\) is the thermal conductivity of the material,\\n  - \\\\( A \\\\) is the area,\\n  - \\\\( \\\\Delta T \\\\) is the temperature difference,\\n  - \\\\( d \\\\) is the thickness of the insulation.\\nIf you double the thickness \\\\( d \\\\), the heat loss \\\\( Q \\\\) is halved, assuming \\\\( \\\\Delta T \\\\) remains constant. This is a standard result in building physics and aligns with the Insulation Outlook Magazine web result, which discusses how insulation reduces heat flow.\\nWall-air temperature differential: Increasing insulation reduces the temperature difference between the wall\\u2019s interior surface and the indoor air because the wall\\u2019s surface temperature approaches the indoor air temperature (less heat is lost to the outside). This is a well-known effect in building science, as noted in the Insulation Outlook Magazine result: insulation impacts the temperature of the air in adjacent spaces, which in turn affects comfort and energy needs.\\nComfort implication: Peltan\\u2019s point that you don\\u2019t need to maintain the same air temperature with better insulation is correct. Warmer wall surfaces (due to better insulation) reduce radiative heat loss from the body, meaning the air can be cooler while maintaining the same level of comfort. This is supported by building science research, such as ASHRAE standards, which emphasize mean radiant temperature (influenced by wall temperatures) as a key factor in thermal comfort.\\nVerdict: This claim is accurate. Doubling insulation does halve conductive heat loss, and it reduces the wall-air temperature differential, improving comfort and reducing the need for high air temperature setpoints.\\n--\\nQualitative Claims About Humidity and Comfort\\nClaim: \\\"Having air that's not overly dry in the winter means it doesn't have to be as warm to be comfortable. Having air that's not overly humid in the summer means it doesn't have to be as cold.\\\"\\nAnalysis:\\nHumidity and comfort: This is correct and aligns with psychrometrics and human comfort studies. In winter, dry air increases evaporation from the skin, making you feel colder (as Peltan notes, evaporation accounts for ~20% of heat loss). Higher humidity reduces this evaporative heat loss, allowing a lower air temperature to feel comfortable. In summer, high humidity reduces the body\\u2019s ability to cool via evaporation, making you feel hotter, so lower air temperatures are needed for comfort.\\nThe web result from ScienceDirect on radiative cooling highlights how humidity impacts cooling performance, supporting Peltan\\u2019s point that humidity control is critical for efficient HVAC design.\\nVerdict: This claim is accurate and consistent with principles of thermal comfort and psychrometrics.\\n--\\nEconomic Value of Insulation\\nClaim: \\\"Insulation provides a much larger efficiency benefit than you'd expect from a simple Manual J calculation. That's compounded by the fact that the economic value of that energy savings is far larger than flat volumetric pricing indicates (because insulation provides the most benefit in extreme conditions where capacity is constrained and prices are high).\\\"\\nAnalysis:\\nManual J calculation: Manual J is a standard method for calculating heating and cooling loads in buildings, primarily focusing on air temperature and conductive\\/convective losses. It often underestimates the impact of radiative heat transfer and humidity, as Peltan notes. By reducing radiative heat loss (via warmer walls) and improving humidity control, insulation can reduce loads more than Manual J predicts, especially in extreme conditions.\\nEconomic value: Peltan\\u2019s argument about the economic value of insulation is tied to peak demand pricing, a recurring theme in his threads (e.g., Thread 2, Post 1841546362862592358). During extreme weather (e.g., Winter Storm Uri, mentioned in Thread 2), electricity prices spike due to constrained grid capacity. Insulation reduces peak demand, which is more valuable than average energy savings under flat pricing. This is supported by the Insulation Outlook Magazine result, which discusses the cost-benefit analysis of insulation in terms of energy savings and temperature regulation.\\nData from the U.S. Department of Energy (as referenced in the initial response) confirms that insulation can reduce heating and cooling costs by 20\\u201350%, with greater savings during peak demand periods.\\nVerdict: This claim is accurate. Insulation\\u2019s benefits extend beyond simple conductive heat loss calculations, and its economic value is indeed higher during peak demand periods.\\n--\\nVisual Evidence (Thermal Images)\\nClaim (implied via images): The thermal images show a person emitting infrared radiation and a cold spot near a door, illustrating radiative heat transfer and poor insulation.\\nAnalysis:\\nThe first image is a thermal scan of a person, showing temperatures around 35\\u00b0C, consistent with skin temperature (34\\u201336\\u00b0C). This supports Peltan\\u2019s point about the body emitting infrared radiation (as calculated earlier at 505 W\\/m\\u00b2).\\nThe second image shows a door with a cold spot at 14.5\\u00b0C (compared to a likely indoor temperature of 20\\u201325\\u00b0C), indicating heat loss due to poor insulation or air leakage. This aligns with Peltan\\u2019s broader argument about underinsulated homes losing heat, increasing energy demand.\\nThese images are qualitative but consistent with the physics of infrared radiation and heat loss.\\nVerdict: The thermal images accurately illustrate the concepts of radiative heat transfer and heat loss through poor insulation.\\n--\\nOverall Assessment\\nJesse Peltan\\u2019s calculations and claims in the post are largely accurate, with a few caveats:\\nRadiative heat flux calculations (505 W\\/m\\u00b2, 364 W\\/m\\u00b2, 545 W\\/m\\u00b2) are correct within rounding errors.\\nHeat loss percentages (60% radiation, 20% evaporation, 15% convection) match established physiological data.\\nHuman body heat output (100 W, 50 W\\/m\\u00b2 over 2 m\\u00b2) is a reasonable approximation, though the net radiative heat loss calculation oversimplifies absorption effects.\\nInsulation\\u2019s impact on energy use (halving energy by doubling insulation) is correct per conductive heat transfer physics.\\nHumidity and comfort claims are consistent with psychrometrics.\\nEconomic value of insulation aligns with peak demand pricing dynamics and building science principles.\\nThe main area where Peltan simplifies is in the net radiative heat loss calculation, where he doesn\\u2019t fully account for the dynamic balance of emission and absorption. However, this simplification doesn\\u2019t undermine his broader argument about the importance of radiative heat transfer and insulation. His qualitative points about building design, humidity control, and economic incentives are well-supported by physics and the context of the related text: Because cooling scales as a square with body surface area, but heat generation scales as a cube with body mass, there is an upper limit to the size of a whale before it gets so hot that it overheats even in near-freezing water!

The physics of heat transfer are way cooler than you think.

If we designed homes with them in mind, they'd be a lot more resilient and energy efficient, and people would be a lot more comfortable.

Insulation standards and HVAC systems are designed around maintaining the air https://t.co/Q5TanV2jN5

If we designed homes with them in mind, they'd be a lot more resilient and energy efficient, and people would be a lot more comfortable. Insulation standards and HVAC systems are designed around maintaining the air temperature inside a building, but air temperature is only a tiny slice of the picture. In typical conditions, only ~15% of the human body's heat loss is from convection. ~60% is from radiation, and ~20% is from evaporation (breathing and sweating). Your body glows like a light bulb with infrared radiation. You are constantly emitting infrared radiation into the environment and absorbing infrared radiation from the environment. Radiative power scales with the 4th power of temperature, so even small changes in temperature result in large changes in heat flux. If your skin temp is 34°C (93.2°F), you emit 505 W/m^2. A wall at 10°C (50°F) emits 364 W/m^2 while a wall at 40°C (104°F) emits 545 W/m^2. Your body is ~100 W and ~2 m^2. You need a net cooling of ~50 W/m^2 to maintain your body temperature. If you weren't constantly bathed in infrared radiation, you'd quickly lose heat to the environment. If you're surrounded by hot surfaces, you can be baked by infrared even if the air temperature is low. The temperature of the walls, ceiling, and floor is more important than temperature of the air. Underinsulated surfaces can be cold even when the air is warm, and hot even when the air is cold. This (and humidity) is why your house at 70°F doesn't feel the same in the winter and the summer. Doubling the amount of insulation in a home cuts the energy required to maintain a given air temperature in half, but it also cuts the differential between the temperature of the walls and the air. With better insulation, not only do you not need as much energy to maintain the same air temperature, you don't even have to maintain the same air temperature. Air conditioning can reduce humidity, but most air conditioners can't intentionally control humidity, and most HVAC system have no way to increase humidity. Better control over humidity reduces the temperature differential your house has to maintain with the outside to provide the same heat transfer for the people inside. Having air that's not overly dry in the winter means it doesn't have to be as warm to be comfortable. Having air that's not overly humid in the summer means it doesn't have to be as cold. The goal is to maintain human body temperature, not air temperature. Radiation and evaporation make up most of the heat transfer. Looking only at air temperature completely misses the big picture. We underinsulate our homes and don't provide adequate control over the aspects that are most important for comfort. Insulation provides a much larger efficiency benefit than you'd expect from a simple Manual J calculation. That's compounded by the fact that the economic value of that energy savings is far larger than flat volumetric pricing indicates. (because insulation provides the most benefit in extreme conditions where capacity is constrained and prices are high) Heat and cooling are the majority of energy use in homes, and the vast majority of energy costs. Real consideration of the physics of heat transfer and the economics of energy systems in the way we design homes and HVAC systems would provide massive economic benefits."}}}, "rest_id": "1919470705906729320", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "13733581", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "1921558140891402657", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "13877729", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

“By enabling high-speed, low-latency and affordable Internet globally, Starlink will do more to educate and lift people out of poverty than any NGO ever.” 

He’s not exaggerating. The data backs him https://t.co/SZtXy7gMqr

For the first time, TBC has continuously mined in a Zero-People-in-Tunnel (ZPIT) configuration. There is nobody in the machine (besides the videographer), which is simultaneously advancing forward and erecting a ring (i.e. building the tunnel). Each full ring weighs ~24,000 pounds. In the same way that full and rapid reusability is the holy grail for rockets, ZPIT continuous mining is the holy grail for Boring Machines.  This is the safest, fastest, and least expensive architecture to build tunnels."}}}, "rest_id": "1922177999472042315", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "19566447", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "1922218417316077984", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "17308232", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

Having children is holding a stake in the future.

Aligns incentives beyond one's own lifetime.

Without this, humans can become temporally greedy and overfit systems to a single generation rather than investing in the future.

text:
Humanity has a shot at reaching the stars, but only if civilization holds strong long enough for Mars to prosper on its own.

Then Mars can come to the aid of Earth, just as America came to the aid of the world in WW1, WW2 and the Cold War.
embedded_text:
Honestly felt like such a special moment to be there to witness that first catch.

Absolutely visceral feeling. Cemented the vibe shift. Humanity is going to make it.

text:
There are 140 laws on the books in South Africa that are explicitly racist against anyone who is not black. 

This is a terrible disgrace to the legacy of the great Nelson Mandela. 

End racism in South Africa now!

The idea here was we'd find general waste, cut spending, execute on a vision of smaller government. And there's certainly a lot of bloat. However, what started out as waste-cutting quickly morphed into understanding why so much money is leaving the federal government and going into NGOs. These NGOs have been responsible for trafficking 20 million illegal immigrants into our country during the Biden years, many of them receiving benefits. And many of these NGOs also are the primary beneficiaries of foreign aid. And then I dig deeper - there is an entire ideology, an entire philosophy connecting this web of foreign aid and mass migration. This is the same ideology which led Congress to finance Ukraine, but not Hurricane Helene victims. This is the same ideology which led us into the Iraq and Afghanistan wars. This is the same ideology that George Soros outlined in his essay, "Toward a New World Order: The Future of NATO." This is the same ideology which caused President Biden to declare 74 million supporters of President Trump the greatest threat to our Republic. To defeat government waste, we have to defeat this ideology. I never expected to set on this path. I don't know where it will head. text:
Anti-white, anti-Indian, anti-Chinese or anti any race laws are not ok!

That is just Apartheid with a new name.
embedded_text:
There are 140 laws on the books in South Africa that are explicitly racist against anyone who is not black. 

This is a terrible disgrace to the legacy of the great Nelson Mandela. 

End racism in South Africa now!

text: The world will be very different in 10 years

What will humans be doing in 10 years?

1. In a decade most people will not be driving. What will we do instead?

2. In a decade most people will have robots in their businesses and probably their homes.

3. In a decade we won't be looking at 2D monitors much anymore.

1. In a decade most people will not be driving. What will we do instead?

2. In a decade most people will have robots in their businesses and probably their homes.

3. In a decade we won't be looking at 2D monitors much anymore.

4. In a decade we will be using a ton of brain/computer interfaces to work and play.

5. In a decade AI will be making us all more productive, and, even, happier. It will be reporting our news. It will be running our businesses. It will be helping us with our health. It will be helping us build. It will help us design new things, new products, new ideas, new lifestyles, new experiences.

6. In a decade we will still be needed to work, but on new things that are hard to imagine today.

7. In a decade we will have dozens of virtual beings in our lives. And your AI will bring new ones into your life depending on your goals. Want to learn Spanish? A new group will show up that are different than the group that will show up if you want to learn Chemistry.

8. In a decade new brain/computer interfaces will be here, and will merge humans with AIs in many ways.

9. In a decade our corporate structures will change to be a hybrid of humans and AIs working together. 

10. In a decade a robot will attend our city council meetings and report to us about what's going on inside.

11. In a decade we won't use applications, like we do today on our iPhones. We will have a singular user interface. On Star Trek you just said "computer do this" and it did. Star Trek will be real in a decade.

12. In a decade we will have armies of robots and drones moving around our cities doing everything from deliveries to giving tours to tourists.

13. In a decade we still will have artists and storytellers, but they will be assisted by AIs to help make that art, and tell that story. AI's will gather other AIs who will warn the humans that someone is telling an interesting story. 

14. In a decade AIs will still be asking humans for assistance. "Hey human can you get a better view of the house that's on fire across the street?"

15. In a decade AIs are assisting humans in coming up with new medical and material breakthroughs. Even designing new computing architectures, whether Quantum, or silicon, or biological, or a hybrid of all three or even something even newer.

I'm already living this way, always talking with Grok. "Hey Grok, what am I missing?"

The number of banks have gone from 28,000 to 4,500

The number of public companies in the US peaked ~8000 and has since shrunk to ~6000

Meanwhile the amount of regulation and regulatory agencies have exploded.  

Regulation, at the limit, strangles

We absolutely should invest in nuclear fission, but even quadrupling our fission capacity is tiny compared to the growth already happening in solar.

300 GW of new nuclear over 25 years is 12 GW/year.

China built 278 GW of solar - last year.

12 GW of new nuclear every year

text: The two fundamental scaling metrics of intelligence are useful compute per Watt and total Watts
embedded_text:
Making the conversion of energy into intelligence as efficient as possible will dramatically drive up the demand for energy and drive us up the Kardashev scale.

We need more thoughts per Watt, this will drive us to produce more Watts.

Whales, as endothermic mammals, produce heat metabolically, and their ability to dissipate this heat to the surrounding environment (typically cold ocean water) determines their maximum size before overheating becomes a problem. Let's break this down:\nKey Factors in Thermal Limitation\nSurface Area-to-Volume Ratio: As a whale grows larger, its volume (and thus heat-producing mass) increases with the cube of its length, while its surface area (through which heat is dissipated) increases only with the square of its length. Larger whales have a lower surface area-to-volume ratio, making it harder to lose heat.\nMetabolic Rate: Whales have high metabolic rates to maintain body temperature in cold water. Larger whales generate more heat due to their greater mass, but their ability to dissipate it is limited by their surface area.\nEnvironment: The cold ocean water (often near 0\u201310\u00b0C) acts as a heat sink, allowing whales to dissipate heat effectively. However, if a whale grows too large, the heat generated internally could exceed the rate of dissipation, leading to overheating.\nInsulation (Blubber): Whales have thick blubber layers for insulation, which reduce heat loss. While this helps them survive in cold water, it also limits their ability to shed excess heat, particularly in larger individuals.\nEstimating the Thermal Limit\nTo determine the maximum size a whale could theoretically reach before being thermally limited, we can consider scaling laws and heat balance:\nHeat Generation: Proportional to body mass, which scales as length\u00b3. For a whale, metabolic heat production can be approximated using Kleiber's law, where metabolic rate scales as mass^(3\/4).\nHeat Dissipation: Proportional to surface area, which scales as length\u00b2. Heat loss occurs primarily through conduction and convection to the surrounding water, governed by the temperature difference between the whale\u2019s body (around 37\u00b0C) and the water.\nThe critical size occurs when heat generation exceeds heat dissipation, leading to a risk of overheating. Mathematical modeling of this balance suggests that whales are thermally limited at sizes approaching or slightly exceeding the largest known whales.\nCurrent Largest Whales\nThe blue whale (Balaenoptera musculus) is the largest animal ever known, reaching lengths of up to 33 meters (108 feet) and weights of 190\u2013200 metric tons. Some exceptional individuals may have approached 250 tons historically. Blue whales are already near the thermal limit for their environment, as their massive size generates significant heat, and their blubber limits heat loss.\nTheoretical Maximum Size\nStudies and biophysical models (e.g., those by researchers like Gearty and Payne) suggest that the thermal limit for whales in typical ocean conditions is close to the size of the largest blue whales. If a whale were to grow significantly larger\u2014say, 40\u201350 meters or 300\u2013400 tons\u2014it would likely face thermal stress unless:\nIt inhabited even colder waters (e.g., polar regions) to enhance heat dissipation.\nIt evolved thinner blubber or other cooling mechanisms (e.g., enhanced blood flow to the skin), which could compromise insulation.\nIt reduced its metabolic rate, which might limit its activity or diving capacity.\nA rough estimate based on scaling laws suggests that a whale could theoretically reach 1.5\u20132 times the mass of a blue whale (around 300\u2013400 tons) before heat dissipation becomes insufficient in typical ocean temperatures (0\u201310\u00b0C). However, this would require evolutionary adaptations to manage heat, such as more efficient heat exchange systems or behavioral changes (e.g., spending more time at the surface).\nPractical Constraints\nBeyond thermal limits, other factors constrain whale size:\nFood Availability: Larger whales require immense amounts of food (e.g., blue whales consume 1\u20132 tons of krill daily). A larger whale might struggle to find enough prey.\nStructural Limits: Bone and muscle strength may limit size, as the skeleton must support the animal\u2019s mass.\nOxygen and Diving: Larger whales need more oxygen for diving, which could limit their foraging efficiency.\nConclusion\nThe largest a whale can be before it is thermally limited is likely close to the size of the largest blue whales (~33 meters, ~200\u2013250 tons). Theoretical models suggest a maximum of around 300\u2013400 tons under optimal conditions (e.g., very cold water, potential adaptations for heat dissipation). Beyond this, overheating would become a significant risk, even in the cold ocean environment. Other ecological and biomechanical constraints likely prevent whales from reaching this theoretical maximum in the biggest a whale can be before it is thermally limited?"}, {"deepsearch_headers": [], "media_urls": [], "message": "The size of a whale is constrained by thermal limits, primarily due to the balance between heat generation and heat dissipation. Whales, as endothermic mammals, produce heat metabolically, and their ability to dissipate this heat to the surrounding environment (typically cold ocean water) determines their maximum size before overheating becomes a problem. Let's break this down: ### Key Factors in Thermal Limitation 1. **Surface Area-to-Volume Ratio**: As a whale grows larger, its volume (and thus heat-producing mass) increases with the cube of its length, while its surface area (through which heat is dissipated) increases only with the square of its length. Larger whales have a lower surface area-to-volume ratio, making it harder to lose heat. 2. **Metabolic Rate**: Whales have high metabolic rates to maintain body temperature in cold water. Larger whales generate more heat due to their greater mass, but their ability to dissipate it is limited by their surface area. 3. **Environment**: The cold ocean water (often near 0–10°C) acts as a heat sink, allowing whales to dissipate heat effectively. However, if a whale grows too large, the heat generated internally could exceed the rate of dissipation, leading to overheating. 4. **Insulation (Blubber)**: Whales have thick blubber layers for insulation, which reduce heat loss. While this helps them survive in cold water, it also limits their ability to shed excess heat, particularly in larger individuals. ### Estimating the Thermal Limit To determine the maximum size a whale could theoretically reach before being thermally limited, we can consider scaling laws and heat balance: - **Heat Generation**: Proportional to body mass, which scales as length³. For a whale, metabolic heat production can be approximated using Kleiber's law, where metabolic rate scales as mass^(3/4). - **Heat Dissipation**: Proportional to surface area, which scales as length². Heat loss occurs primarily through conduction and convection to the surrounding water, governed by the temperature difference between the whale’s body (around 37°C) and the water. The critical size occurs when heat generation exceeds heat dissipation, leading to a risk of overheating. Mathematical modeling of this balance suggests that whales are thermally limited at sizes approaching or slightly exceeding the largest known whales. ### Current Largest Whales The **blue whale** (*Balaenoptera musculus*) is the largest animal ever known, reaching lengths of up to **33 meters (108 feet)** and weights of **190–200 metric tons**. Some exceptional individuals may have approached **250 tons** historically. Blue whales are already near the thermal limit for their environment, as their massive size generates significant heat, and their blubber limits heat loss. ### Theoretical Maximum Size Studies and biophysical models (e.g., those by researchers like Gearty and Payne) suggest that the thermal limit for whales in typical ocean conditions is close to the size of the largest blue whales. If a whale were to grow significantly larger—say, 40–50 meters or 300–400 tons—it would likely face thermal stress unless: - It inhabited even colder waters (e.g., polar regions) to enhance heat dissipation. - It evolved thinner blubber or other cooling mechanisms (e.g., enhanced blood flow to the skin), which could compromise insulation. - It reduced its metabolic rate, which might limit its activity or diving capacity. A rough estimate based on scaling laws suggests that a whale could theoretically reach **1.5–2 times the mass of a blue whale** (around 300–400 tons) before heat dissipation becomes insufficient in typical ocean temperatures (0–10°C). However, this would require evolutionary adaptations to manage heat, such as more efficient heat exchange systems or behavioral changes (e.g., spending more time at the surface). ### Practical Constraints Beyond thermal limits, other factors constrain whale size: - **Food Availability**: Larger whales require immense amounts of food (e.g., blue whales consume 1–2 tons of krill daily). A larger whale might struggle to find enough prey. - **Structural Limits**: Bone and muscle strength may limit size, as the skeleton must support the animal’s mass. - **Oxygen and Diving**: Larger whales need more oxygen for diving, which could limit their foraging efficiency. ### Conclusion The largest a whale can be before it is thermally limited is likely close to the size of the largest blue whales (~33 meters, ~200–250 tons). Theoretical models suggest a maximum of around **300–400 tons** under optimal conditions (e.g., very cold water, potential adaptations for heat dissipation). Beyond this, overheating would become a significant risk, even in the cold ocean environment. Other ecological and biomechanical constraints likely prevent whales from reaching this theoretical maximum in practice."}]}, "is_translatable": false, "legacy": {"bookmark_count": 684, "bookmarked": false, "conversation_id_str": "1921850682816000143", "created_at": "Mon May 12 08:51:23 +0000 2025", "display_text_range": [0, 126], "entities": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [], "timestamps": [], "urls": [{"display_url": "x.com/i/grok/share/6…", "expanded_url": "https://x.com/i/grok/share/6ai1uAzJlGQj1AFAHvRTqyBZe", "indices": [103, 126], "url": "https://t.co/srkaE8evbI"}], "user_mentions": []}, "favorite_count": 19401, "favorited": false, "full_text": "How interesting that we live during a time when the largest creature ever (Blue Whale) is alive too! https://t.co/srkaE8evbI", "id_str": "1921850682816000143", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "possibly_sensitive": false, "possibly_sensitive_editable": true, "quote_count": 150, "reply_count": 1994, "retweet_count": 2555, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "44196397"}, "rest_id": "1921850682816000143", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "10324751", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

embedded_text: This spring at the Salon des Batailles in the Hôtel de Crillon, Nicolas Lerner, head of French intelligence, asked me to ban conservative voices in Romania ahead of elections. I refused. We didn’t block protesters in Russia, Belarus, or Iran. We won’t start doing it in Europe.

text:
As we progress along the Kardashev Scale, energy harnessed on Earth will increase a hundredfold and will mostly be solar aka fusion aka starlight. 

Then energy harnessed will increase perhaps a billionfold if we make it to Kardashev II, with space solar power, and another billionfold if we harness the energy of our galaxy. 

What I’m saying is that there is a lot of upside potential!!!
embedded_text:
A lot of people say we don't need more energy.
We can simply use less and be more efficient.

The 6.3x difference in GDP/kWh between Ireland and Qatar seems to support that, until you notice:
- Ireland's energy use per capita is still 1.7x the global average
- Ireland is a tax https://t.co/o7WeSc2xDS

Total transmission and distribution (T&D) losses are less than 5%. The cost of T&D is NOT LOSSES - it’s capacity. We have to pay the same amount to build and maintain that capacity - regardless of how much we use it. Load (demand) side storage can charge when there’s excess T&D capacity, and discharge when it’s constrained. Batteries improve load factor, which means we can transmit more watt hours through each watt of capacity.
